params.text:
Subject access requests and records rights

People have a legal right to see the personal information held about them. When someone asks for a copy of their own records, this is a subject access request. They are entitled to a copy, normally free of charge, and the organisation must respond without undue delay — usually within one month. You are not expected to handle a request alone or hand over a file on the spot. Your job is to recognise the request, treat it seriously, and pass it promptly to your manager or the responsible person, who will confirm the requester’s identity and make sure information about other people is properly protected before anything is released.

Keeping records only as long as needed

Information should be kept accurate and up to date, and held only for as long as there is a genuine reason to keep it. Organisations follow retention schedules — for health and care records these are set out in the Records Management Code of Practice — which say how long different records should be kept. Keeping information forever ‘just in case’ is not lawful, and neither is destroying records too early. When information does reach the end of its life, it must be disposed of securely: paper shredded or placed in confidential waste, and electronic records deleted so they cannot be recovered. Never simply bin confidential paperwork or leave old devices lying around.

params.question: A person you support says: ‘I want to see everything that is written about me in my care file.’ What is the correct response?

answers.text: Treat it as a subject access request: they have a right to a copy of their information, so pass the request promptly to your manager or the responsible person to handle, usually within one month.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. This is a subject access request; recognising it and escalating it properly is exactly what is expected of you.

answers.text: Refuse, because care records belong to the organisation and people have no right to see what is written about them.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. People have a clear legal right of access to their own personal information; refusing outright is unlawful.

answers.text: Hand over the whole file on the spot yourself, including notes about other people, without telling anyone.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Requests must be handled properly, with identity checked and other people’s information protected before anything is released.

answers.text: Tell them they must pay a large fee and wait at least a year for a decision.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. A copy is normally provided free of charge and the response is usually due within one month, not a year.
